home.intro:
Welcome to the optimization dashboard of the "Offene Wärmespeicherplanung" (OWP) project of the Inno!Nord initiative.

This dashboard can be used to simulate multivalent heat supply systems and the supply units used within them. You can choose from a range of unit types to build your individual heat system. These units are then parameterized, the energy-economic and policy framework conditions are defined, and a combined design and dispatch optimization is performed. The method used is mixed-integer linear optimization (MILP).

### Key Features

- Combined design and dispatch optimization based on [oemof.solph](https://github.com/oemof/oemof-solph)
- Parameterization and results visualization using a [Streamlit](https://github.com/streamlit/streamlit) dashboard
- Wide selection of typical heat supply units
- Extensive database of load data, price time series and emission factors
home.used_software_text:
#### Software used:

The open-source software oemof.solph is used for model creation and simulations. In addition, a number of other Python packages are used for data processing, preparation and visualization.

---

#### oemof.solph:

oemof.solph, as part of the Open Energy Modelling Framework, is a powerful simulation tool for energy systems. The package makes it possible to optimize unit dispatch and design their capacities. The component-based structure combined with generic unit classes provides a high degree of flexibility with regard to system topology and parameterization. Further information on oemof.solph can be found in its [online documentation](https://oemof-solph.readthedocs.io).

#### Additional packages:

- [Streamlit](https://docs.streamlit.io) (graphical user interface)
- [NumPy](https://numpy.org) (data processing)
- [pandas](https://pandas.pydata.org) (data processing)
- [Matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org) (data visualization)
home.publications_text:
[1] Malte Fritz, Jonas Freißmann & Ilja Tuschy. Open Source Web Dashboard zur Unterstützung der Konzeption von Wärmeversorgungssystemen. Tagungsband zur 3. Konferenz der Norddeutschen Wärmeforschung, S. 31-36. Hochschule Bremen, 2026. [doi:10.26092/elib/5016](https://doi.org/10.26092/elib/5016).

[2] Jonas Freißmann, Malte Fritz & Ilja Tuschy. Hochtemperaturwärmepumpen in der Nah- und Fernwärmeversorgung - Technologieperspektive für den kurz- und mittelfristigen Einsatz in multivalenten Systemen. Abschlussbericht, Zentrum für nachhaltige Energiesysteme, 2024. [doi:10.5281/zenodo.13311533](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.13311533).
home.disclaimer_text:
#### Simulation results:

Numerical simulations are calculations using suitable iterative methods based on the specified boundary conditions and parameters. In individual cases, not all possible influences can be taken into account, so deviations from practical experience may occur and should be considered when evaluating the results. Accordingly, all information and results are provided without warranty.

energy_system.misc.lifetime.label: Analysis period in years
energy_system.misc.energy_tax.label: Energy tax in €/MWh

energy_system.misc.mip_gap.label: MIP gap in %
energy_system.misc.time_limit.label: Time limit in minutes
energy_system.heat.description: <small>*This tab defines the data for heat supply. Real generation data (heat load and losses) are available for the cities of Flensburg and Sønderborg. Alternatively, custom hourly time series can be used. For the available datasets, an exact period can be selected. In addition, the data can be scaled to adapt them to individual use cases.*</small>
energy_system.network.description: <small>*This tab defines how heat network costs are considered. You can choose between specific network costs, total network costs or no consideration of network costs. For specific network costs, the route length and specific costs must be specified.*</small>
energy_system.system.description: <small>*This tab is used to select the heat supply units and their number (field next to the respective image). These units are then parameterized in the Units tab.*</small>
energy_system.units.description: <small>*This tab defines the economic and technical parameters of the previously selected heat supply units. The stored values are based on literature data and should be adjusted for the respective simulation if necessary. For each unit, it can also be decided whether the capacity should be optimized. If this option is activated, the economically optimal unit size is determined in addition to optimal operation. If the toggle remains deactivated, the capacity must be specified and only the optimal dispatch of the unit is calculated.*</small>
energy_system.supply.description: <small>*Depending on the previous selections, this tab defines all required data for the respective fuels as well as solar irradiation.*</small>
energy_system.misc.description: <small>*This tab defines other parameters. These include economic and optimization-specific parameters. For the duration of the optimization, the choice of solver and MIP gap are decisive. It is also possible to limit the maximum simulation duration.*</small>
energy_system.supply.solar.description: <small>*You can choose between three predefined locations (North: Schleswig, Central: Chemnitz, South: Stuttgart) and custom datasets. For the available datasets, an exact period can be selected and the data can be scaled if necessary to adapt them to individual use cases. Note: The length of the solar irradiation time series must match the length of the heat load time series.*</small>
energy_system.supply.electricity.descrip: <small>*Both electricity time series and associated emission factors must be defined. For both quantities, variable, constant or custom datasets can be selected. For the predefined data, an exact period can be selected and the time series can be scaled if necessary to adapt them to individual use cases. In addition, the consideration of additional electricity price components can be activated. Note: The length of the electricity price and emission factor time series must match the length of the heat load time series.*</small>
energy_system.supply.gas.description: <small>*Gas price data as well as the associated emission factor and CO₂ price data must be defined. For all quantities, variable, constant or custom datasets can be selected. For the predefined data, an exact period can be selected and the data can be scaled if necessary to adapt them to individual use cases. When selecting the fuel, values for natural gas are stored as defaults, but these can be adjusted accordingly, e.g. for biogas. Note: The length of the gas and CO₂ price time series must match the length of the heat load time series.*</small>
energy_system.heat.error.negative_heat_l: Scaling results in a negative heat load. Please adjust the offset.
energy_system.network.no_costs.warning:
No network costs are considered in the optimization. This means that investment, variable and fixed operating costs for the network connection or for the infrastructure for transporting and distributing heat will not be included in the calculation.

Therefore, the reported heat generation costs represent only the costs of generation within the considered system. If additional network costs were included, the heat generation costs would increase accordingly.
energy_system.error.timeseries_length_mi: The number of time steps in the heat load data ({nr_steps_hl}) does not match those of the {series_name} ({nr_steps_other}). Please align the data.
energy_system.misc.solver.label: Solver
energy_system.misc.solver.highs_unavaila: The `HiGHS` solver is not available on this system. Please use another solver.
energy_system.misc.solver.unavailable: The `{solver}` solver is not available on this system. Please use another solver.
energy_system.error.insufficient_heat_ca:
The selected heat units are not sufficient to cover the maximum heat demand. Possible approaches to solving the problem could be:

- Increase the installed or maximum installable capacity of the units

- Add other units or increase the number of existing units

- Add a thermal storage unit
energy_system.system.select_units.label: Select the heat supply units that can be used in the system.
energy_system.system.unit_count.label: Number of {unit}
optimization.error.no_parameters: **Error:** No parameters are available yet. You must first configure the energy system on the designated page.
optimization.overview.timeseries.subhead: Time series in the heat supply system
optimization.overview.parameters.subhead: Parameters in the heat supply system
optimization.error.infeasible:
The optimization problem could not be solved.

The unit sizes may have been selected such that they are not able to cover the heat load in every time step and/or charge a thermal storage unit.

Possible approaches to solving the problem could be:

- Adjust the installed capacity or the limits of the capacity to be installed for the units

- Add a thermal storage unit

- Add a flexible external heat source
optimization.error.unknown_solver:
An unknown error occurred during optimization.

Check the selected parameters and try to start a new optimization.
results.error.no_results: **Error:** No results are available yet. You must first run an optimization on the designated page.
results.overview.description: <small>*The optimization results are presented in a clear overview. These include the (optimal) capacities, the resulting heat production, and the economic and ecological key figures. After the analysis, a new energy system can either be configured. If the results are to be used for further analyses, a standardized report can be downloaded, or alternatively the complete result data and parameters can be exported.*</small>
results.metric.gas_emissions_t: Emissions from gas consumption in t
results.metric.electricity_emissions_t: Emissions from electricity consumption in t
results.metric.electricity_credit_emissi: Emission credits from electricity production in t
results.unit_commitment.description: <small>*The dispatch results are shown both as ordered annual duration curves and as the actual unit dispatch over the entire analysis period. For more detailed analysis, individual units can be hidden as needed. In addition, it is possible to adjust the analysis period or aggregate the results at different levels in order to evaluate specific questions in a targeted way.*</small>
results.unit_commitment.sorted_duration_: Ordered annual duration curves of unit dispatch
results.aggregation.period.label: Select aggregation period:
results.aggregation.method.label: Select aggregation method:
results.electricity_production.descripti: <small>*The electricity production of the system is shown. In addition to key figures, grid feed-in and internal use of the generated electricity are reported. For better context, the underlying electricity price time series is also visualized. For further analyses, all displayed data can be flexibly aggregated.*</small>
results.electricity_production.feed_in.c: Electricity fed into the grid in MWh
results.storage.description: <small>*The results of the thermal storage units are shown. In addition to key figures, the storage level as well as charging (negative) and discharging (positive) are visualized over time. For deeper analysis, all data can be flexibly aggregated.*</small>
results.advanced.description: <small>*The advanced results contain information for the solver log, which is automatically generated by the respective solver in order to return optimization-specific information for the simulation.*</small>
results.advanced.scip_no_solverlogs: The SCIP solver currently does not provide solver logs.
report.title: Optimization Report: Open Heat Storage Planning
report.charts.offline.title: Charts unavailable
report.charts.offline.text: An internet connection is required to display the charts because the Vega library is loaded from an external server.
charts.axis.weekly_grid_feed_in_mwh: Weekly electricity fed into the grid in MWh
charts.axis.weekly_internal_electricity_: Weekly internally used electricity in MWh
report.template.footer: This report was generated automatically by the OWP MILP optimization software.
tooltips.select_heat_load: Heat load data for the German city of Flensburg or the Danish city of Sønderborg can be selected. Custom data can also be uploaded.
tooltips.own_data_heat_load:
The first column must contain an hourly date index and the second column must contain the heat load in MWh. For CSV files, the delimiter must be a semicolon.

'Date' ; 'heat load' 

 2025-01-01 00:00 ; 50 

 2025-01-01 01:00 ; 45 

 etc.
tooltips.own_data_gas:
The first column must contain an hourly date index, the second column the gas price in €/MWh, and the third column the CO₂ price in €/t CO₂. For CSV files, the delimiter must be a semicolon.

'Date' ; 'gas_price' ; 'co2_price' 

 2025-01-01 00:00 ; 50 ; 40 

 2025-01-01 01:00 ; 60 ; 30 

 etc.
tooltips.own_data_el:
The first column must contain an hourly date index, the second column the day-ahead spot market price in €/MWh, and the third column the emission factor of the electricity mix in kg CO₂/MWh. For CSV files, the delimiter must be a semicolon.

'Date' ; 'spot_market_price' ; 'ef_om' 

 2025-01-01 00:00 ; 70 ; 400 

 2025-01-01 01:00 ; 60 ; 430 

 etc.
tooltips.own_data_ef_el:
The first column must contain an hourly date index and the second column must contain the emission factor of the electricity mix in kg CO₂/MWh. For CSV files, the delimiter must be a semicolon.

'Date' ; 'ef_om' 

 2025-01-01 00:00 ; 400 

 2025-01-01 01:00 ; 430 

 etc.
tooltips.date_picker_heat_load: The exact time period that can be selected depends on the selected year. It is not possible to select a period across the turn of the year.
tooltips.scale_method_hl: The existing load data can be adjusted based on the number of households to be connected, the desired total heat load, a scaling factor, or a compression and offset.
tooltips.scale_households_hl_fl: Number of households in Flensburg: approx. 50,000
tooltips.scale_households_hl_so: Number of households in Sønderborg: approx. 13,000
tooltips.scale_total_hl: Scales the heat load time series proportionally to achieve the specified total load.
tooltips.scale_factor_hl: Multiplies the load data by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_amp_hl: Compresses the load data around the median.
tooltips.scale_off_hl: Shifts the median of the load data.
tooltips.heat_revenue: The achievable heat revenues are typically calculated from a mixed tariff consisting of a capacity price and an energy price, which are set annually.
tooltips.net_dist: Total length of all pipelines in the heat supply network to be investigated. House connections are not included; only main and distribution pipelines as well as material costs are considered.
tooltips.net_inv_spez_mw:
The investment costs incurred for civil engineering, materials, and labor when constructing a heating network, related to the network length in meters.

 Typical investment costs according to the KWW technology catalog:

 Conventional heating network with 110°C supply temperature, paved terrain for civil engineering costs: approx. 1,500 €/m 

 Conventional heating network with 110°C supply temperature, unpaved terrain for civil engineering costs: approx. 1,000 €/m

 Innovative heating network with 70°C supply temperature, paved terrain for civil engineering costs: approx. 1,200 €/m 

 Innovative heating network with 70°C supply temperature, unpaved terrain for civil engineering costs: approx. 350 €/m
tooltips.net_op_cost_fix: The annual fixed costs for maintenance and repair of a heating network, related to the network length in meters. As a rule of thumb, fixed costs in planning are often estimated at around 1% to 2% of the investment costs per year.
tooltips.net_op_cost_var: The variable costs incurred for operating a heating network, related to the accumulated annual heat load in MWh.
tooltips.toggle_balanced: If this is enabled, the dispatch optimization must ensure that the storage content in the last time step is the same as in the first time step.
tooltips.toggle_fix:
If this switch is enabled, the installed capacity must be added to the heating network or storage in every time step (e.g. fixed waste heat from industrial processes, power plants, or data centers).

 Otherwise, the heat flow can be used as needed (e.g. connection to a higher-level district heating network or heat from a wood-fired plant).
tooltips.toggle_invest:
If this is enabled, a combined design and dispatch optimization is performed for this supply unit.

 Otherwise, dispatch is optimized using predefined capacities.
tooltips.input_eta: The efficiency of a heat supply unit describes how much of the primary energy used (e.g. gas or electricity) is actually converted into useful heat.
tooltips.input_eta_th: The thermal efficiency of a CHP unit describes the share of the primary energy input that is converted into useful heat.
tooltips.input_eta_el: The electrical efficiency of a CHP unit describes the share of the primary energy input that is converted into electrical energy.
tooltips.input_cap_N:
The installed capacity corresponds to the heat flow that can be provided at the nominal operating point.

 This parameter is optimized when the *“Optimize capacity”* switch is enabled.
tooltips.input_cap_min: The minimum installable capacity defines the lower limit of the nominal heat flow.
tooltips.input_cap_max: The maximum installable capacity defines the upper limit of the nominal heat flow.
tooltips.input_cop: The COP (Coefficient of Performance) of a heat pump is a measure of its efficiency. It indicates how much heat flow can be supplied in relation to the electrical power used.
tooltips.input_A_N:
The installed collector area specifies the total area of all solar collectors.

 This parameter is optimized when the *“Optimize capacity”* switch is enabled.
tooltips.input_A_min: The minimum installable collector area defines the lower limit of the total area of all solar collectors.
tooltips.input_A_max: The maximum installable collector area defines the upper limit of the total area of all solar collectors.
tooltips.input_eta_col: The collector efficiency describes the efficiency of a solar collector in converting solar radiation into useful heat. It indicates what share of the solar energy incident on the collector is actually converted into heat.
tooltips.input_Q_N:
The installed storage capacity of a thermal energy storage unit describes the maximum amount of heat that the storage can absorb when fully charged.

 This parameter is optimized when the *“Optimize capacity”* switch is enabled.
tooltips.input_Q_min: The minimum installable storage capacity defines the lower limit of the amount of heat that can be stored.
tooltips.input_Q_max: The maximum installable storage capacity defines the upper limit of the amount of heat that can be stored.
tooltips.input_Q_rel_loss: The relative surface heat loss specifies the heat loss per time step in relation to the current storage content.
tooltips.input_init_storage: The initial storage content specifies the relative storage level at the beginning of the simulation (in the first time step).
tooltips.input_Q_in_to_cap: The ratio of charging capacity to storage capacity indicates how much heat can be charged into the storage per unit of time in relation to the maximum amount of heat the storage can hold overall.
tooltips.input_Q_out_to_cap: The ratio of discharging capacity to storage capacity indicates how much heat can be withdrawn from the storage per unit of time in relation to the maximum amount of heat the storage can hold overall.
tooltips.input_inv_spez: Specific investment costs are the total costs of a unit in relation to its size.
tooltips.input_inv_bonus_rel: Relative reduction of the investment costs for this unit. A value of 40% means that the estimated investment costs are only 60% of the value entered in the corresponding field.
tooltips.input_op_cost_var: Variable operating costs are the ongoing annual costs of a unit in relation to its size.
tooltips.input_op_cost_var_sol:
Variable operating costs are the ongoing annual costs of a unit in relation to its size.

For solar thermal systems, these are calculated in the underlying source based on the total amount of heat produced.
tooltips.input_op_cost_fix: Fixed operating costs are all annually recurring costs that arise independently of operating time or energy production.
tooltips.input_op_cost_bonus_rel: Relative reduction of the variable and fixed operating costs for this unit. A value of 40% means that the estimated operating costs are only 60% of the values entered in the corresponding fields.
tooltips.date_picker_el_prices:
The exact time period must match the period of the previously selected heat load.

 Here, too, it is not possible to select a period across the turn of the year.
tooltips.scale_method_el: The existing day-ahead spot market electricity prices can be adjusted using a scaling factor or a compression and offset.
tooltips.scale_factor_el: Multiplies the day-ahead spot market electricity prices by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_amp_el: Compresses the day-ahead spot market electricity prices around the median.
tooltips.scale_off_el: Shifts the median of the day-ahead spot market electricity prices.
tooltips.el_elements:
Electricity price components are the different cost components.

 The final electricity price is composed of the sum of grid purchase or self-consumption charges and the hourly day-ahead spot market prices.
tooltips.date_picker:
The exact time period must match the period of the previously selected heat load.

 Here, too, it is not possible to select a period across the turn of the year.
tooltips.select_sol: The specific irradiance data depend on the latitude of the respective location. They are exemplary for irradiance in northern, central, and southern Germany.
tooltips.scale_method_sol: The existing specific irradiance data can be adjusted using a scaling factor.
tooltips.scale_factor_sol: Multiplies the specific irradiance by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_method_gas: The existing gas prices can be adjusted using a scaling factor or a compression and offset.
tooltips.scale_factor_gas: Multiplies the gas prices by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_amp_gas: Compresses the gas prices around the median.
tooltips.scale_off_gas: Shifts the median of the gas prices.
tooltips.scale_method_co2: The existing CO₂ prices can be adjusted using a scaling factor or a compression and offset.
tooltips.scale_factor_co2: Multiplies the CO₂ prices by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_amp_co2: Compresses the CO₂ prices around the median.
tooltips.scale_off_co2: Shifts the median of the CO₂ prices.
tooltips.scale_method_el_em: The existing emission factors of the electricity mix can be adjusted using a scaling factor or a compression and offset.
tooltips.scale_factor_el_em: Multiplies the emission factors of the electricity mix by the specified value.
tooltips.scale_amp_el_em: Compresses the emission factors of the electricity mix around the median.
tooltips.scale_off_el_em: Shifts the median of the emission factors of the electricity mix.
tooltips.ef_gas:
The emission factor describes the amount of CO₂ emissions released when using 1 MWh of natural gas.

Typical emission factors according to BAFA:

Natural gas: 0.2012 t CO₂ / MWh 

Liquefied petroleum gas: 0.239 t CO₂ / MWh 

Biogas: 0.152 t CO₂ / MWh 

 Biodiesel: 0.070 t CO₂ / MWh 

 Bioethanol: 0.043 t CO₂ / MWh
tooltips.capital_interest: The capital interest rate is a percentage rate that indicates how strongly investments are valued or discounted over time. It reflects the cost of the capital employed, i.e. the “price of money” over the lifetime of an investment.
tooltips.lifetime: The observation period is the period over which costs, revenues, and impacts of a system or project are recorded and balanced.
tooltips.energy_tax: When using fuels and combustible materials, the so-called energy tax applies, which is relevant for gas-fired CHP units and peak-load boilers.
tooltips.vNNE: Avoided grid charges (vNNE) are financial payments received by operators of decentralized energy units because their feed-in avoids or reduces grid load. They are intended to create incentives for decentralized feed-in, as it can relieve the electricity grid.
tooltips.solver:
A solver is used to solve mathematical optimization problems or systems of equations.

Gurobi: License required, but free for teaching/research

SCIP: Open source

HiGHS: Open source
tooltips.MIPGap: The MIPGap parameter controls the minimum quality of the returned solution. It is an upper bound for the actual gap of the final solution.
tooltips.ToggleTimeLimit: If this is enabled, the maximum simulation duration is limited. If the MIPGap has not yet been reached, the current solution is returned, which may be less accurate. In the worst case, no solution may be available.
tooltips.results_design:
These are the installed capacities of the selected heat supply units.

 If *“Optimize capacity”* was selected beforehand, the optimized unit capacity is shown.
tooltips.results_heat_production: Heat production refers to the heat supplied as a result of the optimized dispatch.
tooltips.lcoh_heat:
LCOH (Levelized Cost of Heat) denotes the specific heat generation costs. These are the average costs of providing 1 MWh of heat over the entire lifetime.

These LCOH include only the generation units, not the heat supply network.
tooltips.lcoh_incl_net:
LCOH (Levelized Cost of Heat) denotes the specific heat generation costs. These are the average costs of providing 1 MWh of heat over the entire lifetime.

These LCOH include both the generation units and the heat supply network.
tooltips.rev_heat: Heat revenue results from multiplying the amount of heat consumed by the heat revenue level.
tooltips.rev_el: Revenue can be generated by selling electricity on the spot market.
tooltips.cost_el: These are all electricity costs arising from internal use or grid purchase.
tooltips.cost_el_int: Even when the electricity produced by CHP units is used internally, electricity costs are incurred.
tooltips.cost_el_ext: These are all electricity costs incurred from grid purchase.
tooltips.cost_gas: These are all costs incurred through the use of gas.
tooltips.cost_units:
The total unit costs are the sum of investment costs as well as variable and fixed operating costs of all units in the system.

 The table additionally shows the investment costs as well as variable and fixed operating costs of the individual units.
tooltips.em_ges: Total emissions are composed of the sum of emissions from gas and electricity consumption minus the emission credits from selling electricity.
tooltips.em_gas: Indicates the amount of emissions released through the use of gas.
tooltips.em_el: Indicates the amount of emissions released through the use of electricity.
tooltips.em_spot: By selling electricity on the spot market, an emission credit can be generated in the balance.
tooltips.oadl:
The hourly heat demand and heat production of all heat supply units, sorted in descending order.

 The step width changes depending on the selected aggregation period.
tooltips.toggle_agg_results: If this is enabled, the number of time steps and the aggregation method of the ordered annual duration curve and the daily unit dispatch can be adjusted.
tooltips.agg_method: If an aggregation period other than hourly is selected, the results are either summed or averaged over the aggregation period.
tooltips.adl:
The dispatch optimization results in the unit dispatch for the selected period.

 Negative values represent amounts of heat charged into the thermal storage.
tooltips.el_int: Electricity production (internal) is the amount of internally used electricity produced by the CHP units.
tooltips.el_ext: Electricity production (grid) is the amount of electricity produced by the CHP units and sold on the spot market.
unit_inputs.cap_max.name: Maximum installable capacity
unit_inputs.cap_min.name: Minimum installable capacity
unit_inputs.cap_N.name: Installed capacity
unit_inputs.A_max.name: Maximum installable collector area
unit_inputs.A_min.name: Minimum installable collector area
unit_inputs.A_N.name: Installed collector area
unit_inputs.Q_max.name: Maximum installable storage capacity
unit_inputs.Q_min.name: Minimum installable storage capacity
unit_inputs.Q_N.name: Installed storage capacity
unit_inputs.eta.name: Efficiency
unit_inputs.eta_th.name: Thermal efficiency
unit_inputs.eta_el.name: Electrical efficiency
unit_inputs.eta_col.name: Collector efficiency
unit_inputs.cop.name: COP
unit_inputs.Q_rel_loss.name: Relative surface heat loss
unit_inputs.Q_in_to_cap.name: Ratio of charging power to storage capacity
unit_inputs.Q_out_to_cap.name: Ratio of discharging power to storage capacity
unit_inputs.init_storage.name: Initial storage level
unit_inputs.balanced.name: Balanced storage over the analysis period
unit_inputs.fix.name: Heat must be supplied
unit_inputs.inv_spez.name: Specific investment costs
unit_inputs.inv_bonus_rel.name: Relative investment cost subsidy
unit_inputs.op_cost_var.name: Variable operating costs
unit_inputs.op_cost_fix.name: Annual fixed operating costs
unit_inputs.op_cost_bonus_rel.name: Relative operating cost subsidy
